Citation:
Caldeira DAF, Oliveira DF,
Cavalcanti-de-Albuquerque JP ,
Nascimento JHM, Zin WA and
Maciel L (2021) Isolation of
Mitochondria From Fresh Mice Lung
Tissue Front Physiol 12:748261 doi: 10.3389/fphys.2021.748261
Isolation of Mitochondria From Fresh
Mice Lung Tissue
Dayene de Assis Fernandes Caldeira1†, Dahienne Ferreira de Oliveira2†,
João Paulo Cavalcanti-de-Albuquerque1, Jose Hamilton Matheus Nascimento1,
Walter Araujo Zin1and Leonardo Maciel1,3*
1Carlos Chagas Filho Institute of Biophysics, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil,2Institute of Medical
Biochemistry, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil,3Professor Geraldo Cidade Campus, Federal
University of Rio de Janeiro, Duque de Caxias, Brazil
Direct analysis of isolated mitochondria enables a better understanding of lung
dysfunction Despite well-deﬁned mitochondrial isolation protocols applicable to other
tissues, such as the brain, kidney, heart, and liver, a robust and reproductive protocol
has not yet been advanced for the lung We describe a protocol for the isolation
of mitochondria from lung tissue aiming for functional analyses of mitochondrial O 2
consumption, transmembrane potential, reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation, ATP
production, and swelling We compared our protocol to that used for heart mitochondrial
function that is well-established in the literature, and achieved similar results Keywords: lung mitochondria isolation, O 2-consumption, ROS, ATP, mitochondrial assessment
INTRODUCTION
The assessment of mitochondrial function in organs and tissues is essential for a better
understanding of their biochemistry, physiology, and pathophysiology (Weissig, 2005; Picard
et al., 2011; Meyer et al., 2017; Murphy and Hartley, 2018) The evaluation of mitochondrial
function is usually accomplished in isolated mitochondria (Picard et al., 2011; Gedik et al., 2017;
Maciel et al., 2020, 2021; Caldeira et al., 2021) permeabilized ﬁbers (Perry et al., 2013) or cells
(Perry et al., 2013) These techniques are very well-deﬁned in several types of tissue, e.g., heart
(Gedik et al., 2017), kidney (Schulz et al., 2015), liver (Goudarzi et al., 2018), adipose tissue
(Matta et al., 2021), and brain (Marques Neto et al., 2020), presenting peculiarities and diﬀerent
indications depending on the tissue and the purpose of the investigation However, assessment of
lung mitochondrial function presents diﬃculties associated with obtaining isolated, intact, coupled,
and functional mitochondria The methodological diﬃculty of obtaining viable lung mitochondria
derives mainly from an elevated fatty acid content, low amount of mitochondria in the cell, ﬁbrous
and air-ﬁlled tissue, and the required amount of tissue (Spear and Lumeng, 1978; Kuznetsov
et al., 2008; Lanza and Sreekumaran Nair, 2009) Recently, we have shown that the isolation of
pulmonary mitochondria with preserved structure and function is possible by means of adaptations
of existing techniques and standardization of a speciﬁc method of isolation by diﬀerentiated
centrifugation (Caldeira et al., 2021) However, the diﬀerences related to mitochondrial isolation
procedures for obtaining these well-preserved pulmonary mitochondria have not been addressed
in detail, and the characteristics of reagents and equipment have not been fully described (Caldeira
et al., 2021) Therefore, the main objective of this article is to provide a practical step-by-step
user protocol upgraded to isolate pulmonary mitochondria Our isolation protocol is founded
on the diﬀerentiated centrifugation method of mice lung homogenate However, unlike the
classical procedures currently in use, we will use some innovative steps because of the intrinsic
characteristics of the tissue to obtain better and more functional isolated mitochondria In addition,
we describe in detail the mitochondrial function pertaining to several respiratory complexes Frontiers in Physiology | www.frontiersin.org 1 November 2021 | Volume 12 | Article 748261fphys-12-748261 November 27, 2021 Time: 10:43 # 2
Caldeira et al Mitochondria Isolation From Lung Tissue
MATERIALS AND EQUIPMENT
Materials and Reagents
1 Teﬂon beaker (BRANDRbeaker, PTFE, low form, catalog
number: Z322660; Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) 2 50 ml Falcon tubes (FALCONRBrand, 50 ml polypropylene
conical tube 30 mm 115 mm style, catalog number:
352070; Coring Science Mexico, Col del, Mexico) 3 14 ml round-bottom tubes (Thermo ScientiﬁcTM, NuncTM
14 ml round-bottom tube, catalog number: 150268;
Thermo Fisher ScientiﬁcTM, Waltham, MA, United States) 4 1.5 and 2 ml microfuge tubes (Eppendorf Safe-Lock Tubes,
1.5 and 2 ml Eppendorf QualityTM, catalog numbers:
0030120086 and 0030120094, respectively; Eppendorf,
Hamburgo, Germany) 5 Syringe ﬁlter (CorningRsyringe ﬁlters, nylon membrane,
diameter 25 mm, pore size 0.2 mm, catalog number:
CLS431224; Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) 6
summary: Revised Summary: Researchers from the Carlos Chagas Filho Institute of Biophysics and the Institute of Medical Biochemistry at the Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, along with collaborators from National Taiwan University, Taipei Medical University, University at Buffalo, and University of Coimbra, developed a protocol for isolating mitochondria from fresh mice lung tissue. They conducted functional analyses, including O2 consumption, transmembrane potential, reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation, ATP production, and swelling, to assess mitochondrial function. The study aimed to overcome methodological difficulties associated with the low amount of mitochondria in the lung tissue. The findings contribute to understanding lung dysfunction and provide insights into pulmonary mitochondrial function. The research was published in the journal Frontiers in Physiology.

topics.topic: Isolation of mitochondria from fresh mice lung tissue
topics.justification: The researchers developed a protocol for isolating mitochondria from fresh mice lung tissue, which is the main focus of the study.

The researchers conducted various functional analyses, such as O2 consumption, transmembrane potential, reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation, ATP production, and swelling, to assess mitochondrial function.

topics.topic: Methodological difficulties associated with low amount of mitochondria in lung tissue
topics.justification: The study aimed to overcome methodological difficulties associated with the low amount of mitochondria in the lung tissue, which is an important aspect of the research.
The findings of the study contribute to understanding lung dysfunction, which has implications for respiratory diseases and disorders.
The study provides insights into pulmonary mitochondrial function, which can help in understanding the role of mitochondria in lung health and diseases.

hypothetical_questions.question: How did the researchers overcome the methodological difficulties associated with the low amount of mitochondria in the lung tissue?
What functional analyses did the researchers conduct to assess mitochondrial function in the mice lung tissue?

Imagine you are a researcher trying to study mitochondrial function in lung tissue with low mitochondrial content. How would you modify the protocol developed by the researchers to overcome this challenge?
How can the findings of this study contribute to our understanding of lung dysfunction and its relationship to pulmonary mitochondrial function?
What other types of tissue could benefit from the protocol developed by the researchers for isolating mitochondria? How might this protocol be modified for different tissues?
